ground_truth.title: Vegetable Gardening for Beginners: The Complete Guide
ground_truth.author: Catherine Boeckmann
ground_truth.main_content:
This comprehensive guide covers how to start a vegetable garden from scratch, which vegetables to grow, and when to plant what. The guide includes a starter garden plan consisting of easy-to-grow vegetables, companion planting techniques, and flowers.

Why garden? Most vegetables need 6 to 8 hours of direct sunlight daily, well-draining soil, and nutrient-rich soil mixed with organic matter. Start small—a 10x10-foot garden or a 4x4-foot raised bed is manageable for beginners.

Top 10 easiest vegetables to grow: Lettuce, Green beans, Radishes, Tomatoes, Zucchini, Peppers, Beets, Carrots, Chard/Spinach/Kale, and Peas. Mix in marigolds to discourage pests and attract pollinators.

Cool-season vegetables (lettuce, broccoli, peas) grow in early spring and fall, while warm-season vegetables (tomatoes, peppers, cucumbers) need warm soil in late spring and summer. Plant tall veggies on the north side so they don't shade shorter plants.

The Old Farmer's Almanac Garden Planner is a free online tool that helps plan your garden, calculates proper spacing, pulls in frost dates for your location, and identifies companion plants.
with: Most vegetables need 6 to 8 hours of direct sunlight daily. A few veggies (mostly leafy ones) will tolerate some shade.
It's better to be proud of a small garden than be frustrated by a big one!
Top 10 Easiest Vegetables to Grow: Lettuce, Green beans, Radishes, Tomatoes, Zucchini, Peppers, Beets, Carrots, Chard/Spinach/Kale, and Peas.

Cool-season vegetables such as lettuce, broccoli, and peas grow in the cooler weather of early spring (and fall). Warm-season vegetables such as tomatoes, peppers, and cucumbers aren't planted until the soil warms up in late spring and summer.
The Old Farmer's Almanac Garden Planner automatically calculates the proper spacing for each type of crop and pulls in the frost dates for your specific location.
